issues.title: SCurve and WaterfallChart SVGs lack all ARIA attributes and screen reader support
issues.description: The SVG chart has no role, aria-label, or descriptive text. Screen readers see nothing. Interactive hover points have no keyboard access. This violates WCAG 2.1 AA and the global acceptance standard requiring zero critical axe-core violations.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/SCurve.tsx, in the SCurve component: BEFORE: The root `<svg>` element has no accessibility attributes. Hover interaction on data points is mouse-only with no keyboard support. AFTER: Add `role="img"` and `aria-label="S-Curve chart showing planned versus actual project spend over time"` to the `<svg>` element. Add a `<title>` child element inside the SVG with the same descriptive text. Add a `<desc>` element summarizing the data: e.g., `Planned spend: $47.5M, Actual spend to date: $38.5M`. For the hover state, wrap each data point circle in a `<g>` with `tabIndex={0}`, `role="button"`, `aria-label` describing the data point value, and an `onKeyDown` handler that triggers the same tooltip as `onMouseEnter` when Enter or Space is pressed. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Pay application page has no loading, error, or empty states
issues.description: The getPayApplications function throws raw transformed Supabase errors with no user-friendly messaging. The page acceptance criteria (section 10) require skeleton loaders, empty state with prerequisite check ('You need a schedule of values'), and inline retry on failure. None of these exist.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/payApplications.ts, in the getPayApplications function: BEFORE: The function throws `transformSupabaseError(error)` directly, which surfaces raw database error strings to the UI. There is no distinction between 'no data' and 'fetch failed'. AFTER: Wrap the Supabase query in a try-catch. On error, throw a new Error with a user-friendly message: `'Unable to load payment applications. Please check your connection and try again.'`. When `data` is an empty array, return it normally (the UI component should handle empty state). Add a JSDoc comment to the function documenting the possible return states: empty array (no pay apps), populated array (success), or thrown Error (network/permission failure). Do NOT change the return type signature. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: computeCurrentPaymentDue formula does not match AIA G702 standard for cumulative retainage
issues.description: The retainage calculation applies rate to totalCompletedAndStored (cumulative), but the currentPaymentDue formula subtracts previousCertificates AFTER retainage. Per AIA G702, current payment due = (Total completed and stored to date) - (Retainage) - (Total of previous certificates for payment). The stored materials should NOT have retainage applied. This is a financial calculation bug per the acceptance criteria requiring penny-accurate server-verified math.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/payApplications.ts, in the computeCurrentPaymentDue function: BEFORE: `retainageAmount` is computed as `totalCompletedAndStored * retainageRate`, which incorrectly applies retainage to stored materials. Per AIA G702, retainage applies only to work completed, not to materials presently stored. The current formula is: `retainageAmount = totalCompletedAndStored * retainageRate`. AFTER: Split the retainage calculation: `const retainageOnWork = (previousWork + workThisPeriod) * retainageRate`. Keep `totalCompletedAndStored = previousWork + workThisPeriod + storedMaterials` unchanged. Change `retainageAmount` to equal `retainageOnWork` (not including stored materials). Update `currentPaymentDue = totalCompletedAndStored - retainageOnWork - previousCertificates`. Update the return object to use the corrected `retainageAmount: retainageOnWork`. Add a JSDoc comment above the function documenting the AIA G702 formula: 'Retainage is calculated on work completed only, not on materials presently stored, per AIA Document G702.' After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: WaterfallChart has fixed pixel widths that break on mobile viewports
issues.description: WaterfallChart uses hardcoded barWidth=80 and gap=40 pixel values, creating a minimum width of ~480px that overflows on 375px mobile screens. The acceptance criteria require every page to be usable at 375px with no horizontal scroll. The chart needs responsive sizing.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/WaterfallChart.tsx, in the WaterfallChart component: BEFORE: `barWidth` is hardcoded to `80` and `gap` to `40`, resulting in `totalWidth = bars.length * (80 + 40) + 40 = 520px`. On mobile (375px), this forces horizontal scroll. The outer div has `overflowX: 'auto'` but no responsive adaptation. AFTER: Replace the hardcoded `barWidth` and `gap` with responsive values. Change the chart container to use CSS flexbox with `flex: 1` on each bar wrapper instead of fixed widths. Replace the inline style `minWidth: totalWidth` on the inner div with `width: '100%'`. Change each bar column div from `width: barWidth` to `flex: '1 1 0'` with `minWidth: 50` and `maxWidth: 120`. Change the `gap` in the flex container from `40` to `clamp(8px, 3vw, 40px)` using a CSS calc or inline style with a computed value. Keep the value labels row using the same flex layout. Ensure the connector lines between bars still render correctly by calculating their width dynamically from the actual gap. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Lien waiver status mapping silently drops 'missing' status without user notification
issues.description: The mapDbRow function maps DB status 'missing' to 'pending' silently. The acceptance criteria (section 10) require that missing lien waivers be flagged prominently: 'Cannot submit pay app until all required lien waivers are attached.' This silent mapping hides the 'missing' state from the UI, making it impossible to distinguish between a waiver that was never requested and one that is pending action.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/lienWaivers.ts, in the mapDbRow function: BEFORE: The DB status 'missing' is silently mapped to 'pending' with the comment 'DB missing is legacy for waivers not yet actioned — treat as pending'. This loses the distinction between a waiver that has been requested but not received ('pending') and one that has never been created ('missing'). The UI cannot flag missing waivers per acceptance criteria. AFTER: Add 'missing' to the LienWaiverStatus type union if not already present (check the type definition in `src/types/api.ts` — if it only allows 'pending' | 'received' | 'executed', note this in a TODO comment but keep the mapping for now to avoid a type error). Change the mapping: instead of silently converting 'missing' to 'pending', preserve it as 'missing' if the type allows it, OR add a boolean field `is_missing: boolean` to the mapped output that is `true` when the DB status was 'missing' and `false` otherwise. Add this field to the return object of `mapDbRow`. Update the `mapStatusToDb` function to handle the reverse mapping: if status is 'missing', map it back to 'missing' in the DB. Add a comment: '// Missing waivers must be visually distinct from pending waivers per AIA billing requirements'. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's financial module supports AIA G702/G703 but requires manual PDF generation and lacks earned value analysis. SiteSync's computeEarnedValue engine and EarnedValueDashboard are strong differentiators, but the retainage calculation bug and missing lien waiver visibility gap would erode CFO trust — exactly the audience where the product vision says SiteSync must win. Fieldwire has zero financial tools. Buildertrend has basic billing but no SOV-level detail or retainage tracking. The waterfall chart and treemap visualizations are ahead of Procore's static reports, but they need to work on mobile to match the field-first promise.
summary: The financial engine has solid domain architecture — earned value analysis, waterfall charts, S-curves, AIA pay app calculations, and lien waiver tracking are all present. However, five surgical issues undermine trust and usability: the AIA G702 retainage formula incorrectly applies to stored materials (a penny-accuracy failure that would cost real money), SVG charts completely lack accessibility, pay application endpoints surface raw errors with no loading/empty states, the waterfall chart breaks on mobile, and lien waiver 'missing' status is silently swallowed preventing the critical 'block pay app until waivers collected' workflow. Fixing these five issues would move the module from 47 to approximately 62, with the retainage fix being the highest-value single change because financial accuracy is the core trust differentiator against Procore.
